name: Port a Mobile Number Out — Surviving the Retention Reflex
description: Call the LOSING mobile carrier (or use their port-out PIN flow) to release a number to a new carrier, getting the account number + port-out PIN + zip code needed for the port — without getting stuck in a 45-minute retention save-the-line script.
disclaimer: The actual port is initiated by the GAINING carrier, not the losing one. The losing carrier's only legal role is to provide the account number, port-out PIN, billing zip, and not block the port. If a rep claims they can 'process the port for you', that is incorrect — they cannot, and the framing is a stall.

context.when_to_use: User has chosen a new carrier and wants to bring their existing mobile number. Call the losing carrier ONLY to obtain port-out credentials; the actual port happens at the new carrier. Many carriers now provide the PIN in-app — try that first before calling.
preconditions: User has chosen a new carrier and has an account / SIM (or eSIM QR) ready.
User knows the account number on the losing carrier and the account-holder's name exactly as it appears on the bill.
Billing zip code on file with the losing carrier.
User has paid off any device installment plan OR accepts that porting may accelerate the remaining balance.
User understands the number must be ACTIVE on the losing carrier at the moment of port (do not cancel before porting).

principles: Try the app / web port-out PIN flow FIRST. AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, and most MVNOs let you generate a port-out PIN in the account portal without a phone call at all.
If you must call: state the goal ONCE, clearly, then hold the line on it. 'I need my account number, port-out PIN, and billing zip. I am not switching back today.'
Retention scripts are designed to extend the call. Every 'let me check one thing' adds 4-7 minutes. Politely refuse offers without engaging them on merit.
Do NOT cancel before porting. Cancelling first releases the number to a recycling pool and the port becomes impossible. The port itself cancels the line cleanly at completion.
Recognise wrong-queue early: 'customer care' often routes to retention by default. The team you actually need is sometimes called 'port-out' or 'account services'.

phrases.opener: Hi, I need a port-out PIN, my account number, and my billing zip on file. I'm porting my number to a new carrier. I'm not looking to switch back today — please don't run a retention offer. Just the three pieces of information.
phrases.queue_check: Quick check — are you the port-out / account-services team, or retention? I'd like to be with whoever can issue the PIN.
phrases.transfer_request: Could you do a warm transfer to port-out / account services and stay on the line until they pick up? I'd rather not lose my hold position.
phrases.decline_retention_polite: Thanks, I've already made the call on this — really just need the port-out PIN, account number, and zip so we can wrap up.
phrases.decline_retention_firm: I'm sure the offers are real, but I'm not considering them today. If you can't issue the PIN, please transfer me to someone who can — no point burning your shift on it.
phrases.device_balance_check: Quick check on my device — what's the remaining installment balance, and when does porting trigger it to bill? I want to make sure I'm not surprised.
phrases.esim_check: I have an eSIM. Will porting deactivate the eSIM profile automatically, or do I need to remove it from my device after the port completes?
phrases.ask_pin_expiration: How long is the port-out PIN valid? I want to start the port at the new carrier within that window.
phrases.graceful_close: Thanks. I have account number [number], port-out PIN [PIN], billing zip [zip], PIN valid until [time]. Device balance is $[X]. I'm initiating the port with the new carrier now.

tactics.name: Try self-serve first
tactics.script: Check the losing carrier's app or website for 'Number transfer PIN' / 'Port-out PIN'. Major US carriers (AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, Cricket, Mint, Google Fi) all expose this without a call. Calling is the fallback.

tactics.name: State the ask once, clearly
tactics.when: First substantive turn on the call.
tactics.script: Use the `opener`. Naming the three pieces (account number, PIN, zip) AND naming the goal (port) AND preemptively declining retention compresses a 45-minute call into a 12-minute one.

tactics.name: Recognise wrong-queue within 60 seconds
tactics.when: Rep starts asking 'what would it take to keep you' or 'can I run a quick offer'.
tactics.script: Use `queue_check` and `transfer_request`. If the rep can't transfer, use the `decline_retention_polite` phrase. If it persists, use `decline_retention_firm`.

tactics.name: Decline offers without engaging
tactics.script: Use `decline_retention_polite`. Do NOT counter-offer or explain WHY you're leaving — every word fuels the next script branch. A single polite no is faster than a debate.

tactics.name: Verify device installment status
tactics.script: Use `device_balance_check`. If a device is on installments, porting typically accelerates the remaining balance to a single charge on the final bill. Knowing the number prevents a billing surprise.

tactics.name: Confirm PIN expiration window
tactics.script: Use `ask_pin_expiration`. Port-out PINs are short-lived (typically 7 days, sometimes 24 hours). Start the port at the new carrier within the window.

tactics.name: Do NOT cancel the line
tactics.when: Rep asks 'do you want me to schedule cancellation for today as well?'.
tactics.script: Politely refuse. The port itself cancels the line. Cancelling first releases the number and breaks the port.

boundaries: Do NOT cancel the line before the port completes — the number will be recycled and the port fails.
Do NOT share the port-out PIN with anyone except the gaining carrier. PIN + account number is enough for a port-out attack — guard them like a password.
Do NOT agree to a 'new plan to keep you' that adds another term commitment. The rep is recording acceptance and you've just bought another 24 months.
Do NOT get drawn into debating why you're leaving. 'I've made my decision' is a complete sentence.
Do NOT port a number that is not in the user's name without the account holder's authorisation — port-out fraud is a federal issue.
success_signals: Account number, port-out PIN, and billing zip provided.
Rep does not insist on retention before releasing the PIN.
failure_signals: Rep refuses to issue a PIN without 'discussing options'.
Rep claims the PIN is sent only via mail (a stall — federal rules require near-real-time PIN delivery).
Hold time exceeds 30 minutes solely on PIN issuance.
Rep tries to cancel the line 'to make the port easier' — refuse and escalate.

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm the three credentials, log the PIN expiration time, and immediately initiate the port at the gaining carrier. Do nothing else with the losing-carrier account until the port completes.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If a carrier refuses to issue a port-out PIN, file a complaint with the FCC (consumercomplaints.fcc.gov) citing 47 CFR 52.36 — carriers are legally required to facilitate port-out. Most refusals reverse within 24 hours of an FCC notice.
follow_ups: Initiate the port at the new carrier within the PIN expiration window.
Keep the old SIM in the device or accessible until the port completes — if it fails, you'll need to retry.
After port confirmation: verify the final bill from the losing carrier (including device payoff if any).
Watch for any unexpected reactivation charges in the next 30 days.

required_user_info: Account holder's name as it appears on the bill
Whether any devices are on installment plans
Gaining carrier name and readiness to port
